title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: If you are having an operation, you may be given general anesthesia to push you to sleep

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: and keep you free from pain.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Your doctor may recommend general anesthesia for a procedure that is extensive, takes a long

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: time, or requires you to be in an uncomfortable position.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Before your procedure, an IV line will be placed in a vein in your arm using a small tube

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: called a cannula.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: The IV will deliver fluid and medications directly into your bloodstream.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: You may receive some medication to help you relax.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: You will be placed on the operating table and made as comfortable as possible.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: A blood pressure cuff will be placed on your arm to check your blood pressure readings.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Sticky pads will be placed on your chest to check your heart rate.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: And a clip will be put on your finger to check your body's oxygen levels.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: These devices allow the anesthesia specialist to closely monitor your vital signs before,

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: during and after your procedure.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: You will begin receiving general anesthesia by either breathing anesthetic gases through

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: a mask or through IV injection, which will cause you to fall asleep.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Once you are asleep, you will be given a mixture of oxygen and anesthetic gases either

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: through your mask or through a special tube inserted through your mouth and into your windpipe.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: The tube is attached to a respirator, which helps you breathe while delivering the gases

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: to your lungs.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Deep in your lung tissues, the gases are absorbed into your bloodstream and carried by blood

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: cells to your brain.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: The anesthesia prevents your brain from receiving messages from nerves in your body.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: As a result, you will remain asleep and pain-free during your procedure, and you will have no

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: memory of it when you wake up.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: After your operation, the anesthesia specialist will give you medications to reverse the anesthesia,

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: and you will awaken quickly.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: If you had a breathing tube in place, he or she will remove it as soon as you can safely

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: breathe on your own.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: You will be taken to the recovery room where you will be closely monitored, and given

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: pain medication is needed.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: You may feel light-headed and slightly disoriented, but this feeling should pass quickly.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Once you are stable, you will be sent to a hospital room or home.
